text:
# Page 1^page=1
Monitoring Private 5G Systems with the Asset Administration Shell: A Case Study Mike Reichardt ∗, Michael Gundall ∗, Daniel Buch ∗, Philip Stricker ∗, and Hans D. Schotten∗† ∗German Research Center for Artificial Intelligence (DFKI), Kaiserslautern, Germany †Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, RPTU University Kaiserslautern-Landau, Kaiserslautern, Germany Email: {mike.reichardt, michael.gundall, daniel.buch, philip.stricker hans dieter.schotten}@dfki.de Abstract—Mobile communication technologies are considered fundamental to the implementation of Industry 4.0, leading to a growing deployment of non-public networks. To effectively manage and monitor these networks, digital twins (DTs) have emerged as a promising solution. The Asset Administration Shell (AAS), as a standardized framework for DTs, offers a structured approach to network monitoring and management. While previous research has demonstrated the feasibility of using AAS in simulated envi- ronments, real-world validation remains limited. In this work, we present a technical case study that implements a testbed combining a 5G system with the AAS to monitor license requirements in a non-public network. We evaluate the system’s performance by analyzing responsiveness and demonstrate that the AAS can effectively support real-time monitoring tasks. This study bridges the gap between simulation and practical deployment, highlighting the potential of the AAS for managing 5G network assets in Industry 4.0 contexts. Index Terms—Digital Twin, Asset Administration Shell, Industry 4.0, Wireless Communication I. I N T RO D U C T I O N Industry is currently undergoing a significant transformation, driven by the advancements associated with the Fourth Indus- trial Revolution. This shift is characterized by the integration of various innovative technologies that enable greater automa- tion, connectivity, and intelligent decision-making. Among the key components facilitating this transformation are mobile communication systems, like 5G [1] and the emerging 6G technologies, which offer high-speed, low-latency connections essential for modern industrial applications [2]. Another foun- dational technology is the Digital Twin (DT), which provides a virtual representation of physical assets, allowing for real-time monitoring, simulation, and control [3]. In the context of this industrial evolution, the convergence and mutual influence of core technologies play a crucial role in unlocking new possibilities [4]. A prominent example of this convergence is the integration of DT technology within mobile communication systems, which opens up innovative use cases such as remote maintenance [5]. In fact, recent research has shown that DTs can be utilized in various network management tasks, helping to maintain the smooth and efficient operation of communication infrastructures [6]. This highlights the critical importance of continuous network monitoring. A considerable body of research focuses on leveraging the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) as a DT technology, largely due to its standardized metamodel, which facilitates consistent and interoperable implementations [7]. The AAS has been employed in several domains, including predictive maintenance [8] and change management [9]. Middleware solutions such as Eclipse BaSyx provide an AAS-compliant client-server archi- tecture with APIs that support effective system monitoring. Recent simulation-based studies have demonstrated that net- work management using the AAS is theoretically feasible [10]. However, there remains a gap in the literature concerning the practical performance of such approaches in real-world mobile networks, particularly in operational 5G environments. In our research, we address this gap by evaluating the responsiveness and latency of a real 5G system when used in conjunction with a network management framework based on the AAS and its middleware. The following contributions can be found within this paper: • Challenges of monitoring private networks • Monitoring private networks with the AAS • Validating the case study, based on the performance time The structure of this paper is as follows. Section II reviews existing research on the integration of 5G technologies with DTs, with a particular focus on the AAS in industrial applications. Section III introduces the case study and outlines the developed system architecture. Section IV details the methodology used for time measurement and defines the metrics employed to eval- uate the system’s performance. Section V presents the results and demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed approach through numerical evaluation. Finally, Section VI concludes the paper and provides an outlook on future research directions. II. R E L AT E D W O R K DTs have increasingly been recognized as enablers for 5G and future communication systems, offering new dimensions of integration and control in industrial contexts [5], [6]. For instance, Nguyen et al. identify DTs as valuable tools for emulation within 5G automotive scenarios, enabling continu- ous optimization, validation, and the realization of new use cases [11]. Building upon these foundational ideas, recent research explores the development of network DTs to simulate and emulate network behavior with higher fidelity [12], [13]. As DT technologies evolve, the need for standardized repre- sentations becomes increasingly relevant in mobile communi- cations. One proposed solution involves utilizing the AAS to structure and manage a wireless communication system [14]. The 5G Alliance for Connected Industries and Automation (5G- ACIA) has introduced the concept of a 5G-AAS architecture,

# Page 2^page=2
which comprises both 5G User Equipment (UE) AAS and 5G Network AAS entities [15]. This concept has been further developed by Gomez et al., who implemented both the 5G UE AAS and 5G Network AAS as structured Submodels (SMs) [16]. However, these implementations are not officially certified by the International Digital Twin Association (IDTA) and are not currently listed as available Submodel Templates (SMTs) on the official IDTA platform. Cainelli et al. present an alternative use of the AAS for dy- namic management of uplink and downlink resource slots, based on current application requirements [14]. Through simulation, they demonstrate that such an approach can meet production- level demands in industrial settings. Follow-up work extends this idea to the broader management of 5G non-public networks, also called private networks, using the AAS, including regula- tory considerations critical to such deployments [17]. This line of research supports the feasibility of AAS-based management of industrial 5G networks, aligning with evolving operational requirements. Despite these promising developments, most contributions remain limited to simulation environments. To date, there is a lack of studies that evaluate these approaches in real-world testbeds with a focus on measurable system performance, par- ticularly in terms of reaction time. The present work addresses this gap by providing a case study that empirically assesses the achievable performance of a 5G-enabled system using AAS- based integration. III. C A S E S T U DY A. 5G Network Monitoring with Asset Administration Shells 5G systems represent a fundamental advancement in indus- trial communication, offering high bandwidth, low latency, and reliable connectivity for time-critical applications. In particu- lar, non-public 5G networks, or private network called, have emerged as a key enabler for flexible, localized communica- tion infrastructure in industrial environments. However, the introduction of these networks poses new challenges for in- dustrial companies, for example due to licensing requirements. To legally operate private networks, organizations must obtain and maintain campus licenses, which are contingent upon the compliance of various network parameters with regulatory constraints. Consequently, continuous monitoring of license- relevant parameters is essential to avoid violations that could lead to the suspension or loss of operational licenses. Beyond compliance, monitoring also plays a vital role in ensuring optimal network performance under dynamic condi- tions. Informed by prior research on network operation and the integration of AAS [17], the presented case study employs the AAS framework to monitor the maximum transmission power 𝑃tx of the 5G system1 This specific parameter was selected for several reasons, including its direct link to licensing constraints and its ability to be updated at runtime without requiring a full system restart, which accelerates the measurement process. 1Code for the application and measurements can be found on Gitlab: https: //github.com/dfki-in/Monitoring-Private-5G-Systems-with-the-AAS A primary objective of this case study is to assess whether the system is capable of handling rapid updates to 𝑃tx, which is es- sential for real-time or latency-sensitive industrial applications. Therefore, the system’s reaction to changes in transmission power is measured at various points in the data processing chain. The analysis focuses on four key timestamps: the time at which the system confirms that 𝑃tx has been updated (𝑇update), the moment the change is detected at the base station (𝑇bsout), the point at which the data enters the BaSyx environment (𝑇databridge), and finally, the time at which BaSyx registers the update and emits a corresponding event (𝑇event). The methodol- ogy for generating and capturing these timestamps is described in detail in Sec. III-D. Upon the occurrence of 𝑇event, an application within the system is triggered to evaluate whether the current transmission power complies with the licensing constraints. If the applica- tion detects a violation, it initiates a feedback mechanism by instructing the 5G system to adjust 𝑃tx accordingly to reestablish compliance. While the response time of the application logic can vary significantly depending on the specific industrial scenario and its complexity, no direct time measurements are performed for this component within the scope of this study. Nonetheless, the full case study, including the application, is made available as part of the accompanying source code to support reproducibility and further research. B. System Architecture The system architecture described in this case study integrates two distinct but interconnected systems: a 5G communication system and a Factory Cloud platform (see Fig. 1). These systems are designed to communicate with each other through a RESTful API, enabling a reliable exchange of data between the network infrastructure and industrial applications. The 5G system itself is composed of two core components: a 5G core network and an indoor base station. These two elements communicate with each other over a secure SSH connection, allowing for remote configuration and control while maintaining system integrity and security. The 5G system provides network- level metrics and status information, which are made accessible to the Factory Cloud via the REST interface. The Factory Cloud includes an OPC UA server, which acts as a bridge between industrial communication standards and the 5G system. Network information received via REST is for- warded over TCP to the BaSyx Databridge, which incorporates a built-in OPC UA client. The Databridge is responsible for relaying this information into the broader BaSyx system through a REST-based interface, where it is stored and processed. Specifically, the received data is written into the Monitoring SM and visualized in Fig. 2. The BaSyx architecture is modular and containerized, consisting of multiple dedicated services. The data is initially processed in the AAS environment, then registered in the Registry, and finally stored in MongoDB. Each of these services runs in its own isolated container. The registered AAS instances and their SMs can be explored through the AAS GUI, which is also hosted in a separate container.

# Page 3^page=3
Factory Cloud TextMQTT Publish 5G System SSH 5G Core Network OPC UA TCP OPC UA Server Basyx MQTT Pub/Sub App gNB Publish MQTT Event AAS Enviroment Registry AAS GUI MQTT Broker Databridge REST API OPC UA Client REST API MongoDB OPC UA TCP OPC UA Client bidirectional Databridge REST API Fig. 1: System architecture for network management of a 5G system that is connected through a REST API to a Factory Cloud that uses the AAS and Eclipse Basyx. To enable fast interaction, the AAS environment publishes events to a Mosquitto MQTT broker whenever changes occur. This allows a connected App to react to these updates by sending relevant monitoring data back into the BaSyx system and specifically into the associated Monitoring SM. Two return paths are available for this feedback: either directly via the REST API, as illustrated in Fig. 1, or through the BaSyx Databridge. Given the characteristics of the current monitoring use case where the App sends data infrequently, the REST API is the preferred method, as it imposes a lower network load compared to the Databridge. However, due to the current unidirectional nature of the BaSyx Databridge, the feedback from the app is relayed from the MQTT broker to the OPC UA server via an OPC UA client over TCP. This extra step compensates for the lack of bidirectional communication in the Databridge. In future iterations, this step can be eliminated by deploying a bidirectional Databridge, as indicated by the dashed arrow in Fig. 1. This enhancement would enable the Databridge to natively transmit information back to the OPC UA server, thereby streamlining the data flow from the AAS environment to the OPC UA server. As a result, it facilitates flexible and bidirectional data integra- tion between the factory floor and the cloud-based digital twin infrastructure. C. Hardware and Software Environment The corresponding hardware setup can be seen in Tab.I, and the software components in Tab.II. The 5G system consists of a 5G Core operating on an Intel Xeon D-2123IT processor (2.2 GHz), while the gNB is implemented using a Secromm Bridg- Stone SCE5164. The 5G Core leverages MECsware software (version: MECS-3.16.1/927). The Factory Cloud is hosted on a Fig. 2: UML-Diagram of the Monitoring Submodel, containing all relevant parameters, including 𝑃TX. system running Ubuntu 24.04 LTS and is equipped with an Intel Xeon E-2146G CPU (3.5 GHz) and an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 Super GPU, which are used for the time measurements. The software environment includes Python, with the asyncua library serving as the basis for the OPC UA server implementa- tion. The AAS infrastructure is built on Eclipse BaSyx (version: 2.0.0-SNAPSHOT), which provides containerized services for the GUI, environment, and registry. For persistent storage of AAS data, BaSyx integrates a MongoDB database (version: 5.0.10). To enable communication between the OPC UA server and BaSyx, the BaSyx Databridge (version: 1.0.0-milestone-08) is employed. Additionally, Eclipse Mosquitto (version: 2.0.15) serves as the MQTT broker, which facilitates messaging for the Monitoring App, itself implemented in Python. D. Timestamp Creation All timestamps in this study were measured in milliseconds since the Unix Epoch (1 January 1970 UTC), ensuring a consis- tent temporal format across the system. To maintain temporal coherence, all timestamps were generated on the same physical

# Page 4^page=4
Tab. I: Hardware Components Used in the Setup Component Group Role / Subsystem Details 5G System 5G Core Hardware Intel Xeon D-2123IT @ 2.20GHz 5G System gNB Secromm BridgStone SCE5164 Factory Cloud Host CPU Intel Xeon E-2146G @ 3.50GHz Factory Cloud Host GPU NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 SUPER Tab. II: Software Components Used in the Setup Component Group Role / Subsystem Details Version / Identifier 5G System 5G Core (Software) MECsware 5G Core MECS-3.16.1/927 Factory Cloud Operating System Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS OPC UA OPC UA Server (Python) asyncua 1.1.6 BaSyx Infrastructure AAS GUI Eclipse BaSyx AAS GUI 2.0.0-SNAPSHOT BaSyx Infrastructure AAS Environment Eclipse BaSyx AAS Env 2.0.0-SNAPSHOT BaSyx Infrastructure AAS Registry Eclipse BaSyx Registry 2.0.0-SNAPSHOT BaSyx Infrastructure Databridge Eclipse BaSyx Databridge 1.0.0-milestone-08 Persistence Layer Database MongoDB (Docker Image) 5.0.10 Messaging Layer MQTT Broker Eclipse Mosquitto (Docker Image) 2.0.15 Application Layer Monitoring App Custom Python Script Internal Version machine, using the same system clock. Multiple timestamp points were introduced to capture specific events across the data flow. The timestamp 𝑇update was created on the OPC UA server and marks the initiation of a 5G system update via a REST API. Here, the measuring point was defined as the arrival of the first bit of the REST response. This timestamp was calculated by capturing the absolute time using Python’s time module immediately before sending the REST request and then adding the measured duration until the arrival of the response. The timestamp 𝑇bsout was also created on the OPC UA server using the Python time module, recorded at the moment the server updated its variables. The Basyx Databridge generated the timestamp 𝑇databridge by injecting a timestamp into each update using a JSONata transformer with the $millis() function. The application-level timestamp 𝑇event was recorded directly in the app using the Python time module immediately after a message was received. All generated timestamps were stored in PostgreSQL tables. IV. M E T H O D O L O G Y Assessing the performance of the introduced system architec- ture requires the definition and application of suitable evaluation metrics. In the context of this work, latencies observed at various processing and communication stages within the system are considered the most informative indicators of performance. These metrics offer insights into how quickly information prop- agates through the system components and highlight areas with potential for optimization. The structure and timing of the data flow are illustrated in Fig.3. As part of the methodology, four timestamps are gener- ated during the system’s operation: 𝑇update, 𝑇bsout, 𝑇databridge, and 𝑇event. These timestamps, introduced and explained in Sec.III-D, allow for the definition of distinct latency intervals associated with different functional segments of the system. The first derived metric is the time 𝑡bu, which represents the duration from the completion of the update process until the system confirms that this update has occurred. This metric captures the latency introduced by the 5G system’s internal data propagation and notification mechanisms. It is calculated as 𝑡bu = 𝑇bsout − 𝑇update (1) The second metric, 𝑡db, measures the time it takes for the information about the completed update to travel from the system boundary to the databridge. This includes both the com- munication through the OPC UA protocol and initial processing within the databridge. It is defined as 𝑡db = 𝑇databridge − 𝑇bsout (2) The third metric, 𝑡ed, captures the time interval from the reception of the update by the databridge to the point at which BaSyx has fully processed the new value and triggered an event. This value reflects the performance of the databridge and the event-handling capabilities of the BaSyx framework. The corresponding formula is 𝑡ed = 𝑇event − 𝑇databridge (3) In order to evaluate the system’s end-to-end responsiveness, the total time from the beginning of the update process to the generation of the final event within BaSyx is calculated. This total time, denoted as 𝑡total, can be expressed as the sum of the individual latency components: 𝑡total = 𝑡bu + 𝑡db + 𝑡ed = 𝑇event − 𝑇update (4) To isolate the influence of the 5G system from the evaluation, an additional metric, 𝑡wo, is introduced. This metric excludes the initial detection phase handled by the 5G interface and

# Page 5^page=5
5G System OPC UA Databridge Basyx Ptx updated published notification REST PUT Client request REST PATCH Server response MQTT Event publish App MQTT Event arrived GET response REST GET Tupdate Tbsout Tdatabridge Tevent tbu tdb ted Fig. 3: Timeline of the case study from 5G system to the Application. Showing every timestamp and the calculated times. instead focuses on the performance of the communication and processing layers beyond the system’s edge. It is defined as 𝑡wo = 𝑡db + 𝑡ed = 𝑇event − 𝑇bsout (5) The three base metrics, called𝑡bu, 𝑡db, and 𝑡ed, represent latency at critical transition points in the system. The metric 𝑡bu is inher- ently dependent on the 5G system in use, including the mecha- nism by which it communicates updates to external systems. In this study, the 5G system communicates through a REST-based interface, as outlined in the system architecture in Sec. III. In contrast, 𝑡db includes latency associated with OPC UA commu- nication and the initial stage of event translation performed by the databridge, while 𝑡ed captures the processing and notification time within the BaSyx infrastructure. Analyzing these metrics independently helps to identify which stage contributes most significantly to the overall system delay, thereby directing optimization efforts more effectively. The total time 𝑡total provides an aggregate measure of system responsiveness, which is crucial for evaluating the feasibility of time-sensitive applications. However, since this metric in- corporates the 5G communication stack, its values may vary significantly with the 5G implementation. The inclusion of 𝑡wo offers a valuable abstraction that allows for performance comparison across different system architec- tures and monitoring solutions. This makes it possible to bench- mark the used configuration against alternative frameworks such as FA3ST or other digital twin infrastructures. The 𝑡wo metric thus serves as a standardized indicator for comparing time-critical behavior across heterogeneous system designs, independent of the underlying 5G communication model. V. R E S U LT S A N D VA L I DAT I O N To validate the performance and behaviour of the system under investigation, a total of 𝑛 = 1033 measurements were col- lected. However, during the evaluation process, it was observed that 33 of these measurements were negatively influenced by Tab. III: Measured time intervals for 1000 measurements. Variable Mean [ms] Median [ms] Standard Deviation [ms] 𝑡bu 19564.70 19386.50 1619.77 𝑡db 51.42 50.00 27.76 𝑡ed 8.38 9.00 3.39 𝑡total 19624.49 19457.00 1619.75 𝑡wo 59.97 59.00 28.36 external factors and had to be discarded. The effective dataset consists of 𝑛 = 1000 valid and usable measurements, used in the analysis. The primary reason for the corrupted or invalid measurements was the excessive number of GET requests being sent to the system. This high-frequency querying caused the system to become unresponsive, and requiring a system reboot to restore functionality. The recorded time intervals can be found in Tab. III. The data reveal that the update detection time, denoted as 𝑡bu, is particularly critical. This time parameter is heavily influenced by the latency introduced by the 5G communication system, which is responsible for relaying state changes and update signals. On average, the detection time 𝑡bu is 19.56 s. In addition to this substantial delay, the standard deviation associated with this measurement is 1619 ms, which indicates a considerable variation in detection times and suggests that the system lacks consistency in its responsiveness. This variability is further illustrated in Fig. 4a, which presents a histogram of the measured 𝑡bu values alongside a fitted normal distribution curve. In this figure, the x-axis represents the binned measurement intervals, while the y-axis shows the frequency of occurrence as a percentage. The histogram shows that the majority of measurements fall within a relatively narrow band between 17 and 21 s, suggesting a central tendency. However, several notable outliers exist, particularly around 25 s, which contribute to the overall standard deviation. These delays are

# Page 6^page=6
𝜇 = 19.56s 𝜎 = 16.19s 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 5 15 25 35 45 𝑡bu [s]𝑃[%] (a) For the update detection 𝑡bu. 𝜇 = 51.41 ms 𝜎 = 27.76 ms 0 15 30 45 60 75 90 105 0 1 2 3 4 5 𝑡db [ms]𝑃[%] (b) For the time from OPC UA to the databridge 𝑡db. 𝜇 = 8.38 ms 𝜎 = 3.39 ms 0 5 10 15 20 0 6 12 18 𝑡ed [ms]𝑃[%] (c) For the BaSyx operational time 𝑡ed. 𝜇 = 19.62s 𝜎 = 16.19s 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 𝑡total [s]𝑃[%] (d) For the total time needed 𝑡total. 𝜇 = 59.79 ms 𝜎 = 28.36 ms 0 15 30 45 60 75 90 105 0 1 2 3 4 5 𝑡wo [ms]𝑃[%] (e) For the time without the 5G system 𝑡wo. Fig. 4: Distribution based on 1000 measurements and the corresponding normal distribution curve. attributed to the REST-based interface of the 5G system, which requires several seconds per request-response cycle. Since the system relies solely on this interface for status queries, there are currently no viable alternatives to reduce latency or bypass this bottleneck. The overall system response time, 𝑡total, further emphasizes the dominant role played by the 5G system. The average value of 𝑡total is 19.62 s, with a standard deviation of 1619 ms, differing by only 0.02 ms from 𝑡bu. This minimal difference demonstrates that the update detection time accounts for the vast majority of the total operational delay. Consequently, the histogram shown in Fig 4d closely mirrors that of Fig.4a, reinforcing the conclusion that the 5G system is the principal source of latency within the architecture. In contrast to the 5G-dependent stages, other time measure- ments within the system are confined to the millisecond range. These include the communication time from the OPC UA server to the databridge, which exhibits an average latency of 51.42 ms and a median of 50 ms. The distribution is asymmetrical, with slightly more outliers exceeding the median than falling below it, as indicated by the relatively high standard deviation of 27.76 ms. Fig. 4b shows that the values are fairly evenly distributed between 2 ms and 103 ms, with no strong clustering around any single value range. This suggests that although the communication is generally fast, its determinism could be improved. Furthermore, the BaSyx internal operation time, measured from the arrival of data at the databridge until the correspond- ing event is triggered, shows significantly lower latency. This parameter, denoted as 𝑡ed, averages 8.38 ms and has a median of 9 ms. Interestingly, the data reveal a tendency for faster-than- average executions, as there are more outliers on the lower end of the distribution. The standard deviation of this stage is 3.39 ms. Fig. 4c shows a relatively concentrated frequency between 10 ms and 12 ms, indicating that most executions fall within this narrow time band despite a few fast outliers. Excluding the influence of the 5G system, the combination of 𝑡db (databridge communication) and 𝑡ed (BaSyx processing) gives a composite measure representing the latency internal to the factory cloud. This combined time has an average of 59.97 ms and a median of 59 ms, with a standard deviation of 28.36 ms, that is slightly higher than that of 𝑡db. Fig. 4e depicts the distribution of these values. The curve shows an almost equal distribution, resulting from the superposition of the two individual time components. However, due to its higher variance and wider spread, 𝑡db remains the dominant factor in this superposition. It is important to note that 𝑡total, though theoretically a sum of all three time components, is in practice overwhelmingly dictated by the latency introduced by the 5G system. This is a critical finding, as it suggests that even though the factory cloud infrastructure is capable of fast responses, the management interface of the 5G system used within this case study limits the system’s overall responsiveness. Based on these results, several conclusions can be drawn. First, the currently deployed 5G system does not offer a low-

# Page 7^page=7
latency management plane. This is caused by long and highly variable REST-Calls. This does not allow for real-time adjust- ments of the 5G system. In contrast, the factory cloud and its internal communication systems operate reliably within the millisecond range, offering the responsiveness required for tasks such as process monitoring or feedback control. Nonetheless, some fluctuation of up to 100 ms is still observed, which should be taken into account during system design. To overcome the identified limitations, another 5G system should be considered. Here, 5G systems are available that allow communication on the management interface based on publish-subscribe paradigms, such as Apache Kafka, offering significantly lower latencies. Using such 5G systems, the factory cloud could fully leverage its capabilities, enabling efficient monitoring and potentially even control tasks in industrial environments. VI. C O N C L U S I O N In this case study, we proposed and implemented a system architecture combining an industrial-grade 5G communication system with the AAS using the BaSyx framework. The system was evaluated by collecting timestamps at key stages of the data flow, including the completion of the update message, detection of the changed maximum transmission power by the factory cloud, integration of the information into the BaSyx Databridge, and the emission of an event by BaSyx with the updated value. The evaluation showed that the factory cloud can operate with a latency of approximately 60 ms, measured from the OPC UA server to the BaSyx eventing system, enabling fast response times and the monitoring of time-critical data. This confirms the viability of using AAS for the timely monitoring of network parameters. However, performance limitations were identified, primarily due to the REST-based API provided by the 5G system. The polling-based approach introduced delays and increased network load due to high-frequency GET requests. Alternative 5G systems offering data pipelines (e.g., Kafka) or Pub/Sub show potential to overcome these limitations by enabling event-driven communication with lower latency and reduced load. Future work should investigate alternative 5G systems, such as 5G systems using data pipelines, to evaluate their suitability for real-time industrial monitoring and control using AAS-based frameworks. Another promising direction for future work involves managing a 5G network with multiple UEs through the AAS. This includes dynamically updating network requirements based on the individual needs of different UEs at specific points in time.
